# Dinky

> A tiny macOS image compression app. Converts JPGs and PNGs to WebP, AVIF, or lossless PNG. Drag and drop, get smaller files back. Free, open source, and entirely local — images never leave your machine.

## What it does

- Converts JPG, PNG, WebP, AVIF, TIFF, BMP → WebP, AVIF, or lossless PNG
- Drag and drop or file picker; batch compression with live results
- Max width resizing (common web presets or custom value)
- Max file size targeting via binary search on quality
- Smart quality: detects photos vs screenshots via Vision framework + EXIF, adjusts quality per content type
- Finder Quick Actions — compress without opening the app
- Manual mode: drop files, then right-click each one to pick format individually
- Performance modes: Fast (serial) or Fastest (all cores)
- Update checker: polls GitHub Releases on launch, shows banner if newer version exists

## Technical details

- macOS 15 Sequoia or later (liquid glass UI on macOS 26 Tahoe; frosted material fallback on Sequoia)
- SwiftUI + AppKit, zero SPM dependencies
- 4.7 MB installed
- Compression engines bundled: cwebp (Google), avifenc (AOM), oxipng (Rust/MIT)
- No cloud, no accounts, no telemetry (site uses Fathom analytics, app has none)
- Open source: MIT license

## Comparison

- vs **ImageOptim**: ImageOptim is lossless-only — it squeezes a JPEG and returns a JPEG. Dinky converts formats, where the real savings are (30–80% vs same-format squeeze).
- vs **TinyPNG**: TinyPNG uploads images to their servers. Dinky is 100% local.
- vs **Squoosh**: Browser-based, one image at a time. Dinky is native, batch, offline.
- vs **Optimage**: Similar feature set but Optimage costs $69 and weighs 62 MB. Dinky is free and 4.7 MB.
